questions.question: A perfectly rigid body is one:
questions.question_raw: A perfectly rigid body is one:

options.label: A
options.text: which starts flowing like water on the application of force.

options.label: B
options.text: which does not move on the application of force.

options.label: C
options.text: whose shape and size do not change on application of force.

options.label: D
options.text: whose shape and size change on application of force.

correct_option_texts: whose shape and size do not change on application of force.
questions.explanation: A perfectly rigid body is hypothetical in nature but for some phenomena ( in rotational bodies ) we assume bodies are perfectly rigid i.e. the intermolecular forces are always in equilibrium irrespective of the external forces due to which their shape and size are constant.

questions.question: Elasticity is the property of a body, by virtue of which:
questions.question_raw: Elasticity is the property of a body, by virtue of which:

options.label: A
options.text: it changes size and shape when the force is applied and stays in that shape when the applied force is removed.

options.label: B
options.text: it tends to regain its original size and shape when the applied force is removed.

options.label: C
options.text: it is distorted or stretches without the application of force.

options.label: D
options.text: it remains in original size and shape when the force is applied.

correct_option_texts: it tends to regain its original size and shape when the applied force is removed.
questions.explanation: When an external force is applied to the solid bodies, the solid bodies get deformed. The atoms or molecules are displaced from their equilibrium positions causing a change in the interatomic (or intermolecular) distances. When the deforming force is removed, the interatomic forces tend to drive them back to their original positions. Thus the body regains its original shape and size.

questions.question: For the earth the escape speed in terms of g, the acceleration due to gravity and R_E, the radius of the earth is
questions.question_raw: For the earth the escape speed in terms of g, the acceleration due to gravity and RE, the radius of the earth is
questions.explanation:
The work done to displace a body from the surface of earth (r = R_e) to infinity ( $r = \infty $ ) is
$W = G{{Mm} \over {R_e{\rm{ }}}}$
If v_e is the required escape velocity then kinetic energy which should be given to the body is ${1 \over 2}{\rm{ }}m{v_e}^2$
$\therefore {1 \over 2}{\rm{ }}m{v_e}^2 = G{{Mm} \over R_e}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_e} = \sqrt {2g{R_e}} $

questions.question: The importance of the elastic behavior of materials is:
questions.question_raw: The importance of the elastic behavior of materials is:

options.label: A
options.text: that it is useful in building sling shots.

options.label: B
options.text: that it enables a safe and sound design of bridges, buildings, machinery parts.

options.label: C
options.text: that it gives methods for understanding materials.

options.label: D
options.text: that it is useful in making springs.

correct_option_texts: that it enables a safe and sound design of bridges, buildings, machinery parts.
questions.explanation: More the elastic a material is, more it has the property to regain its original position which is required in construction works.

questions.question: When a solid is deformed:
questions.question_raw: When a solid is deformed:

options.label: A
options.text: only the atoms or molecules at some points move from their equilibrium position.

options.label: B
options.text: the atoms or molecules do not move from their equilibrium position.

options.label: C
options.text: only the atoms or molecules of the surface move from their equilibrium position.

options.label: D
options.text: all the atoms or molecules are displaced from their equilibrium positions causing a change in interatomic (or intermolecular) distances.

correct_option_texts: all the atoms or molecules are displaced from their equilibrium positions causing a change in interatomic (or intermolecular) distances.
questions.explanation: External force permanently disturbed the equilibrium position of the interatomic (or intermolecular) forces between the particles of solid bodies.

questions.question: Stress is:
questions.question_raw: Stress is:
questions.explanation: Stress is defined as restoring force per unit area.

questions.question: The S.I. unit of stress is:
questions.question_raw: The S.I. unit of stress is:
questions.explanation:
As Stress = $Force \over Area$
Putting units, we get
Stress = N/m^2
= Nm^-2 or Pascal 
So SI Unit of Stress is Pascal

questions.question: If a wire has an initial length L and becomes L + $\Delta {\rm{L}}$ on the application of force, the longitudinal strain is given by:
questions.question_raw: If a wire has an initial length L and becomes L + {tex}\Delta {\rm{L}}{/tex} on the application of force, the longitudinal strain is given by:
questions.explanation: The longitudinal strain is defined as the ratio of change in length $\Delta$L and natural length L i.e. longitudinal strain = $\frac{\triangle L}L$

questions.question: Referring to figure shearing strain is defined as:
questions.question_raw: Referring to figure shearing strain is defined as:
questions.explanation:
Shear strain is measured as a change in angle between lines that were originally perpendicular.Thus from the above figure
Shearing strain $ = \frac{{\Delta x}}{L}$
and in a triangle, $\frac{{\Delta x}}{L} = \tan \theta $
thus shearing strain = tan$ \theta $

questions.question: The volumetric strain is defined as K:
questions.question_raw: The volumetric strain is defined as K:

options.label: A
options.text: as the ratio of change in volume ($\Delta $V) to thrice the original volume V.

options.label: B
options.text: as the ratio of change in volume ($\Delta $V) to the original volume V.

options.label: C
options.text: as the ratio of change in volume ($\Delta $V) to twice the original volume V.

correct_option_texts: as the ratio of change in volume ($\Delta $V) to the original volume V.
questions.explanation:
By definition of volumetric strain, it is the ratio of change in volume of the body to its original volume 
= $\Delta V \over V$
Hence volume strain is defined 
as the ratio of change in volume ($\Delta $V) to the original volume V.

questions.question: According to Hooke’s law:
questions.question_raw: According to Hooke’s law:

options.label: A
options.text: for small deformations, the stress is proportional to the square of strain.

options.label: B
options.text: for large deformations, the stress and strain are proportional to each other.

options.label: C
options.text: for small deformations, the stress and strain are inversely proportional to each other.

options.label: D
options.text: for small deformations, the stress and strain are proportional to each other.

correct_option_texts: for small deformations, the stress and strain are proportional to each other.
questions.explanation: By definition of Hooke's law within the elastic limit, the stress developed is directly proportional to the strain produced in a body stress $\propto $ strain.

questions.question: With reference to figure the elastic zone is:
questions.question_raw: With reference to figure the elastic zone is:
questions.explanation: In region 'oa' strain is very small and stress is proportional to the strain. In this region the Hooke's law is valid i.e. if the applied force is removed at any point between ' o ' to 'a' the body will acquire its natural length. Thus this region is called the elastic zone.

questions.question: With reference to figure the plastic zone is:
questions.question_raw: With reference to figure the plastic zone is:
questions.explanation:
The plastic zone represents where the body does not regain its original shape and size.
This is due to crossing of the elastic limit beyond the yield point. 
Hence the plastic zone should be 'cd'.

questions.question: With reference to figure the yield point, corresponds to:
questions.question_raw: With reference to figure the yield point, corresponds to:
questions.explanation: Beyond point 'c' in the wire starts showing an increase in the strain without an increase in stress. Thus, the point 'c' at which the wire yields to the applied stress and begins to flow down is called the yield point.

questions.question: With reference to figure the yield, strength point corresponds to:
questions.question_raw: With reference to figure the yield, strength point  corresponds to:
questions.explanation:
The yield point is a point on the curve where the body deforms with a negligible amount of stress.
Hence it should be B

questions.question: Elastomers are materials:
questions.question_raw: Elastomers are materials:

options.label: A
options.text: which can be stretched without corresponding stress

options.label: B
options.text: which cannot be stretched to cause large strains

options.label: C
options.text: which can be stretched to cause large strains

options.label: D
options.text: which cannot be stretched to beyond the elastic limit

questions.explanation: Substances like tissue of aorta (Present in the heart), rubber, etc. which can be stretched to cause large strains are called elastomers. They show a large plastic range in the stress-strain curve.

questions.question: Young’s modulus Y is:
questions.question_raw: Young’s modulus Y is:

options.label: A
options.text: the ratio of shear stress $\left( \sigma \right)$ to the longitudinal strain $\left( {\theta {\rm{ }}} \right)$.

options.label: B
options.text: the ratio of shear strain $\left( {\theta {\rm{ }}} \right)$ to the longitudinal stress $\left( \sigma \right)$.

options.label: C
options.text: the ratio of longitudinal strain $\left( {\varepsilon {\rm{ }}} \right)$ to the tensile (or compressive) stress $\left( \sigma \right)$.

options.label: D
options.text: the ratio of tensile (or compressive) stress $\left( \sigma \right)$ to the longitudinal strain $\left( {\varepsilon {\rm{ }}} \right)$.

correct_option_texts: the ratio of tensile (or compressive) stress $\left( \sigma \right)$ to the longitudinal strain $\left( {\varepsilon {\rm{ }}} \right)$.
questions.explanation:
The ratio of stress and strain is called modulus of elasticity is the characteristic of the material. they are (a) Young's modulus (b) Shear Modulus (c) Bulk modulus
Young's Modulus is denoted by Y and is given by the ratio of tensile (or compressive) stress $\left( \sigma \right)$ to the longitudinal strain $\left( {\varepsilon {\rm{ }}} \right)$ 
Y = tensile stress/longitudinal strain
Y = $ \sigma \over \epsilon$

questions.question: Shear modulus or modulus of rigidity is:
questions.question_raw: Shear modulus or modulus of rigidity is:

options.label: A
options.text: the ratio of shearing stress to the corresponding shearing strain.

options.label: B
options.text: the ratio of shearing strain to the corresponding shearing stress.

options.label: C
options.text: the ratio of shearing stress to the corresponding lateral strain.

options.label: D
options.text: the ratio of longitudinal stress to the corresponding shearing strain.

correct_option_texts: the ratio of shearing stress to the corresponding shearing strain.
questions.explanation:
The ratio of shearing stress to the corresponding shearing strain is called the shear modulus of the material and is represented by G. It is also called the modulus of rigidity.
G = $shearing \ stress (\sigma) \over shearing \ strain$

questions.question: Bulk modulus is:
questions.question_raw: Bulk modulus is:

options.label: A
options.text: the ratio of longitudinal stress to the corresponding hydraulic strain.

options.label: B
options.text: the ratio of lateral stress to the corresponding hydraulic strain.

options.label: C
options.text: the ratio of hydraulic strain to the corresponding hydraulic stress.

options.label: D
options.text: the ratio of hydraulic stress to the corresponding hydraulic strain.

correct_option_texts: the ratio of hydraulic stress to the corresponding hydraulic strain.
questions.explanation:
When a body is submerged in a fluid, it undergoes hydraulic stress (equal in magnitude to the hydraulic pressure). This leads to a decrease in the volume of the body thus producing a strain called volume strain. The ratio of hydraulic stress to the corresponding hydraulic strain is called bulk modulus. It is denoted by symbol B.
Hence, bulk modulus = hydraulic or normal stress/ volumetric strain
$B = {- p\over ({\delta V \over V})}$ 
The negative sign indicates the fact that with an increase in pressure, a decrease in volume occurs.

questions.question: For a rope of yield strength S_y loaded in tension with weight Mg the minimum area A of the rope should be:
questions.question_raw: For a rope of yield strength Sy loaded in tension with weight Mg the minimum area A of the rope should be:
questions.explanation:
We know that Yield strength times the Area gives the weight of the body.
As Yield strength = $\frac{\text { weight of body }}{\text { area }}$
For safety purposes
Yield strength $ \geq $ $\frac{\text { weight of body }}{\text { area }}$
${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{y}}}$ $ \geq $ $\frac{Mg}{A}$
A $ \geq $ $\frac{Mg}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{y}}}$

questions.question: For a bar of length l, breadth b, and depth d as shown in the figure the deflection $\delta $ is given by:
questions.question_raw: For a bar of length l, breadth b, and depth d as shown in the figure the deflection {tex}\delta {/tex} is given by:
questions.explanation: From $\delta=\frac{W l^3}{\left(4 b d^3 Y\right)}$ , we see that to reduce the bending for a given load, one should use material with a large Young's modulus Y. For a given material, increasing the depth d rather than the breadth b is more effective in reducing the bending, since $\delta$ is proportional to d^-3 and only to b^-1. But on increasing the depth, unless the load is exactly at the right place, the deep bar may bend as shown in the question. To avoid this, a common compromise is the cross-sectional shape is chosen. This shape reduces the weight of the beam without sacrificing the strength and hence reduces the cost.

questions.question: Material is said to be ductile if:
questions.question_raw: Material is said to be ductile if:

options.label: A
options.text: the material cross-section is not significantly reduced at failure.

options.label: B
options.text: the material breaks suddenly at little elongation.

options.label: C
options.text: a large amount of plastic deformation takes place between the elastic limit and the fracture point.

options.label: D
options.text: a fracture occurs soon after the elastic limit is passed.

correct_option_texts: a large amount of plastic deformation takes place between the elastic limit and the fracture point.
questions.explanation: If the ultimate strength and fracture points are close in the stress-strain curve, the material is said to be brittle. If they are far apart, the material is said to be ductile and show a large plastic range.

questions.question: Material is said to be brittle if:
questions.question_raw: Material is said to be brittle if:

options.label: A
options.text: a fracture occurs soon after the elastic limit is passed.

options.label: B
options.text: the material cross-section is significantly reduced at failure.

options.label: C
options.text: a large amount of plastic deformation takes place between the elastic limit and the fracture point.

options.label: D
options.text: material elongates a lot before finally breaking.

correct_option_texts: a fracture occurs soon after the elastic limit is passed.
questions.explanation: If the ultimate strength and fracture points are close, it means a very small plastic range beyond the elastic limit, the material is said to be brittle.

questions.question: A steel wire of length 4.7 m and cross-sectional area 3.0 $ \times $ 10^-5 m^2 stretch by the same amount as a copper wire of length 3.5 m and cross-sectional area of 4.0 $ \times $ 10^−5 m^2 under a given load. What is the ratio of Young’s modulus of steel to that of copper?
questions.question_raw: A steel wire of length 4.7 m and cross-sectional area 3.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-5 m2 stretch by the same amount as a copper wire of length 3.5 m and cross-sectional area of 4.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 m2 under a given load. What is the ratio of Young’s modulus of steel to that of copper?
questions.explanation:
Length of the steel wire, L_1 = 4.7 m
Area of cross-section of the steel wire, A1 = 3.0 $\times$ 10^-5 m^2
Length of the copper wire, L_2 = 3.5 m
Area of cross-section of the copper wire, A_2 = 4.0 $\times$ 10^-5 m^2
Change in length = $\Delta$L_1 = $\Delta$L_2 = $\Delta$L
Force applied in both the cases = F
Young’s modulus of the steel wire:
Y_1 = ($F_1 \over A_1$) ($L_1 \over \Delta L_1$)
= ($F \over 3 \times 10^{-5}$) ($4.7 \over \Delta L$) ….(i)
Young’s modulus of the copper wire:
Y_2 = ($F_2 \over A_2$) ($L_2 \over ΔL_2 $)
= ($F \over 4 \times 10^{-5}$) ($3.5 \over \Delta L$) ….(ii)
Dividing (i) by (ii), we get:
$Y_1 \over Y_2$ = $(4.7 \times 4 \times 10^{-5}) \over (3 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.5)$
= 1.79 : 1
The ratio of Young’s modulus of steel to that of copper is 1.8:1.

questions.question: A steel rod 2.0 m long has a cross-sectional area of 0.30 cm^2. It is hung by one end from a support, and a 550-kg milling machine is hung from its other end. Determine the stress on the rod?
questions.question_raw: A steel rod 2.0 m long has a cross-sectional area of 0.30 cm2. It is hung by one end from a support, and a 550-kg milling machine is hung from its other end. Determine the stress on the rod?
questions.explanation:
Stress = $F\over A$
F = m $\times$ a
F = 550Kg $\times$ 9.8m/s^2 = 5390N
A = 0.30cm^2 = 0.30 $\times$ 10^-4
Stress = $F\over A$
Stress = $5390\over 0.30$ $\times$ 10^-4 = 1.8 $\times$ 10^8Pa

questions.question: A steel rod 2.0 m long has a cross-sectional area of 0.30 cm^2. It is hung by one end from a support, and a 550-kg milling machine is hung from its other end. Determine the resulting strain.
questions.question_raw: A steel rod 2.0 m long has a cross-sectional area of 0.30 cm2. It is hung by one end from a support, and a 550-kg milling machine is hung from its other end. Determine the resulting strain.
questions.explanation:
young′s modulus y = $\frac{\text {stress}}{\text {strain}}$
$\Rightarrow$Strain = $\frac{\operatorname{stress}}{y}$
stress = $\frac{\text {restoring force}}{\text {area}}$
In a given case restoring force will be tension, which will be equal to the weight ′mg′ of the machine. using the standard value of young modulus for steel, y = 2 $\times$ 10^11N/m^2
strain = $\frac{m g}{A \times y}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{550 \times 9.8 \times 10^{4}}{0.30 \times 2 \times 10^{11}}$ = 8.9 $\times$ 10^−4 $\approx$ 9.0 $\times$10^−4

questions.question: A steel rod 2.0 m long has a cross-sectional area of 0.30 cm^2. It is hung by one end from a support, and a 550-kg milling machine is hung from its other end. Determine the elongation. Take Young's modulus of steel as 20 $ \times $ 10^10 Pa?
questions.question_raw: A steel rod 2.0 m long has a cross-sectional area of 0.30 cm2. It is hung by one end from a support, and a 550-kg milling machine is hung from its other end. Determine the elongation. Take Young's modulus of steel as 20 {tex} \times {/tex} 1010 Pa?
questions.explanation:
young′s modulus y = $\frac{{{\text{stress}}}}{{{\text{strain}}}}$ $\Rightarrow \text {strain}=\frac{\text {stress}}{y}$
$\operatorname{stress} =\frac{\text {restoring force}}{\text {area}}$
in a given case restoring force will be tension, which will be equal to the weight ′mg′ of the machine. Using the standard value of young modulus for steel y = 2 $\times$ 10^11N/m^2
strain = $\frac{m g}{A \times y} \Rightarrow \frac{550 \times 9.8 \times 10^{4}}{0.30 \times 2 \times 10^{11}}$ = 8.9 × 10^−4 $\approx$ 9.0 $\times$ 10^−4
for given case strain will be longitudinal i.e. $\Delta$LL $\Rightarrow$ $\Delta$LL = 9 $\times$ 10^ - 4(given L = 2.0m) $\Rightarrow$ $\Delta$L = 2.0 $\times$ 9.0 $\times$ 10^−4 $\Delta$L = 1.8mm

questions.question: A hydraulic press contains 0.25 m^3 (250 L) of oil. Find the decrease in the volume of the oil when it is subjected to a pressure increase $\Delta {\rm{p}}$ = 1.6 $ \times $ 10^7 Pa(about 160 atm or 2300 psi). The bulk modulus of the oil is B = 5.0 $ \times $ 10^9 Pa?
questions.question_raw: A hydraulic press contains 0.25 m3 (250 L) of oil. Find the decrease in the volume of the oil when it is subjected to a pressure increase {tex}\Delta {\rm{p}}{/tex} = 1.6 {tex} \times {/tex} 107 Pa(about 160 atm or 2300 psi). The bulk modulus of the oil is B = 5.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 109 Pa?
questions.explanation:
$\Delta$V = $-V_0\Delta p\over B$ = $-(0.25 m^3 \times 1.6 \times 10^7 Pa)\over5.0 \times 10^9 Pa $
= −8.0 $\times$ 10^-4 m^3 = − 0.80L = -8.0 $\times$10^-4 m^3 = -0.80L
Even through the pressure increase is very large, the fractional change in volume is very small.
∴△V/V_0 = $-8\times 10^{-4}\over 0.25$ = -0.0032 or -0.32%

questions.question: Two wires of diameter 0.25 cm, one made of steel and the other made of brass are loaded as shown in Figure. The unloaded length of steel wire is 1.5 m and that of brass wire is 1.0 m. Compute the elongation of the brass wire.
questions.question_raw: Two wires of diameter 0.25 cm, one made of steel and the other made of brass are loaded as shown in Figure. The unloaded length of steel wire is 1.5 m and that of brass wire is 1.0 m. Compute the elongation of the brass wire.
questions.explanation:
Diameter of the wires, d = 0.25 m
Hence, the radius of the wires, r = $\frac{d}{2}$ = 0.125 cm
Length of the steel wire, L_1 = 1.5 m
Length of the brass wire, L_2 = 1.0 m
total restoring force acting on brass wire i.e. tension T_2 = m_2g = 6g
Total restoring force exerted on the steel wire:
T_1 = m_1g + T_2 = (4 + 6) g = 10 $\times$ 9.8 = 98 N
Total restoring force on the brass wire:
T_2 = 6 $\times$ 9.8 = 58.8 N
Young’s modulus for brass: Y_2
$\Delta$L_2 = Change in the length of the brass wire
A_2 = Area of cross-section of the brass wire = $\pi$r_2^2
Y_2 = $\frac{stress}{strain}$ = $\frac{T_{2} \times L_{2}}{A_{2} \times \Delta L_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta L_{2}=\frac{58.8 \times 1.0}{3.14 \times\left(0.125 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2} \times 0.91 \times 10^{11}}$
Elongation of the brass wire = 1.3 $\times$ 10^–4 m.

questions.question: The edge of an aluminum cube is 10 cm long. One face of the cube is firmly fixed to a vertical wall. A mass of 100 kg is then attached to the opposite face of the cube. The shear modulus of aluminum is 25 GPa. What is the vertical deflection of this force?
questions.question_raw: The edge of an aluminum cube is 10 cm long. One face of the cube is firmly fixed to a vertical wall. A mass of 100 kg is then attached to the opposite face of the cube. The shear modulus of aluminum is 25 GPa. What is the vertical deflection of this force?
questions.explanation:
Edge of the aluminium cube, L = 10 cm = 0.1 m
The mass attached to the cube, m = 100 kg
Shear modulus ($\eta$) of aluminium = 25 GPa = 25 $\times$ 10^9 Pa
shear modulus ($\eta$) = $\frac{shear\;stress}{shear\;strain}=\frac{T\times L}{A\times\Delta L}$
Where,
T = restoring force = Applied force(F) = mg = 100 $\times$ 9.8 = 980 N
A = Area of one of the faces of the cube = 0.1 $\times$ 0.1 = 0.01 m^2
$\Delta$L = Vertical deflection of the cube
shear modulus ($\eta$) = $\frac{shear\;stress}{shear\;strain}=\frac{T\times L}{A\times\Delta L}$
$\Delta L=\frac{T \times L}{\eta \times A}=\frac{980 \times 0.1}{10^{-2} \times 25 \times 10^{9}}$
$\therefore $ $\Delta$L = 3.92 $\times$ 10^−7m $\approx $ 4.0 $\times$ 10^−7m

questions.question: A circular steel wire 2.00 m long must stretch no more than 0.25 cm when a tensile force of 400 N is applied to each end of the wire. What minimum diameter is required for the wire?
questions.question_raw: A circular steel wire 2.00 m long must stretch no more than 0.25 cm when a tensile force of 400 N is applied to each end of the wire. What minimum diameter is required for the wire?
questions.explanation:
Let Young's modulus of steel wire Y
$Y=\frac{s t r e s s}{s t r a i n}=\frac{T \times L}{A \times \Delta L}=$
$A=\frac{T \times L}{Y \times \Delta L}=\frac{400 \times 2.0}{2.0 \times 10^{11} \times 0.25 \times 10^{-2}}$
$\pi$R^2 = 1600 $\times$ 10^−9 .....(A = $\pi$R^2)R = 7.13 $\times$ 10^−4m
diameter D = 2R = 2 $\times$ 7.13 $\times$ 10^−4
D = 1.427 $\times$ 10^−3m$\approx$1.43mm

questions.question: A metal rod that is 4.00 m long and 0.500 cm^2 in cross-sectional area is found to stretch 0.20 cm under a tension of 5000 N. What is Young’s modulus for this metal?

yv = 2.0 $\times$ 10^11N/m^2

questions.question: In constructing a large mobile, an artist hangs an aluminum sphere of mass 6.0 kg from a vertical steel wire 0.50 m long and 2.5 $ \times $ 10^−3 cm^2 in a cross-sectional area. On the bottom of the sphere, he attaches a similar steel wire, from which he hangs a brass cube of mass 10.0 kg. Compute the tensile strain?
questions.question_raw: In constructing a large mobile, an artist hangs an aluminum sphere of mass 6.0 kg from a vertical steel wire 0.50 m long and 2.5 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−3 cm2 in a cross-sectional area. On the bottom of the sphere, he attaches a similar steel wire, from which he hangs a brass cube of mass 10.0 kg. Compute the tensile strain?
questions.explanation:
Given mass of aluminium sphere m_sph = 6.0 KG

questions.question: In constructing a large mobile, an artist hangs an aluminum sphere of mass 6.0 kg from a vertical steel wire 0.50 m long and 2.5 $ \times $ 10^−3 cm^2 in a cross-sectional area. On the bottom of the sphere, he attaches a similar steel wire, from which he hangs a brass cube of mass 10.0 kg. Compute the elongation show calculations for strain in the upper wire?
questions.question_raw: In constructing a large mobile, an artist hangs an aluminum sphere of mass 6.0 kg from a vertical steel wire 0.50 m long and 2.5 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−3 cm2 in a cross-sectional area. On the bottom of the sphere, he attaches a similar steel wire, from which he hangs a brass cube of mass 10.0 kg. Compute the elongation show calculations for strain in the upper wire?

length of steel wire L_steel = 0.50 m
area of steelwire A_steel = 2.5 $\times$ 10^−3 cm^2
mass of cube m_cube = 10kg
net restoring force in lower wire i.e. tension T_2 will be equal to weight of cubei.e.T_2 = m_cube g = 10 $\times$ 9.8 N
net restoring force inupperwirei.e.tensionT_1

standard value for young modulus of steel y = 2 $\times$ 10^11N/m^2
$y=\frac{\text {tensile stress}}{\text {tensile strain}}$
for lower wire 
$\text {strain}_{l w}=\frac{\text {tensile stress}}{y}$ = $\frac{10 \times 9.8}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11}}$
strain_lw = 1.96 $\times$ 10^−3 $\approx$ 2.0 $\times$ 10^−3
$\frac{\Delta L_{l w}}{L l_{w}}=2.0 \times 10^{-3} $ $\Rightarrow \Delta L_{l w}=0.50 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$
$\Delta $L_lw = 1.0 mm $\rightarrow$ (1) 
now for upper wire
strain_up = $\frac{\text {tensile stress}}{y}$ = $\frac{16 \times 9.8}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11}}$

questions.question: A specimen of oil having an initial volume of 600 cm^3 is subjected to a pressure increase of 3.6 $ \times $ 10^6 Pa and the volume is found to decrease by 0.45 cm^3 what is the bulk modulus of the material?
questions.question_raw: A specimen of oil having an initial volume of 600 cm3 is subjected to a pressure increase of 3.6 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 Pa and the volume is found to decrease by 0.45 cm3 what is the bulk modulus of the material?
questions.explanation:
Bulk modulus is defined as B = $- P\Delta V \over V$
here P is volume stress which is equal to the pressure 
given P = 3.6 $\times$ 10^6 pa
$\Delta$V = −0.45cm^3
V = 600cm^3
$B=-\frac{3.6 \times 10^{6}}{-0.45 \over 600}$
B = 4.8 $\times$ 10^9pa

questions.question: A specimen of oil having an initial volume of 600 cm^3 s subjected to a pressure increase of 3.6 $ \times $ 10^6 Pa and the volume is found to decrease by 0.45 cm^3 what is the compressibility of the material?
questions.question_raw: A specimen of oil having an initial volume of 600 cm3 s subjected to a pressure increase of 3.6 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 Pa and the volume is found to decrease by 0.45 cm3 what is the compressibility of the material?
questions.explanation:
Compressibility (K) is defined as reciprocal of bulk modulus
bulk modulus is defined as B = $−P\over {\Delta V\over V}$
here P is volume stress which is equal to pressure given P = 3.6 $\times$ 10^6pa
$\Delta$V = −0.45cm^3
V = 600cm^3
$B=-\frac{3.6 \times 10^{6}}{-0.45 / 600}$
B = 4.8 $\times$ 10^9 pa
K = 1/B = $1\over 4.8 \times 10^9$ = 2.1 $\times$ 10^-10 Pa^-1

questions.question: A copper cube measures 6.00 cm on each side. The bottom face is held in place by very strong glue to a flat horizontal surface, while a horizontal force F is applied to the upper face parallel to one of the edges. How large must F be to cause the cube to deform by 0.250 mm? (shear modulus of copper = 4.4 $ \times $ 10^10 Pa)
questions.question_raw: A copper cube measures 6.00 cm on each side. The bottom face is held in place by very strong glue to a flat horizontal surface, while a horizontal force F is applied to the upper face parallel to one of the edges. How large must F be to cause the cube to deform by 0.250 mm? (shear modulus of copper = 4.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 1010 Pa)
questions.explanation:
Shear modulus ($\eta$) is given as η = $\frac{\text {shear stress}}{\text {shear strain}}=\frac{F \over A}{x \over h}$ $\rightarrow$ (1)
given for copper $\eta$ = 4.4 $\times$10^10pa x = 0.250mm h = 6.0cm
side of cube a = 6.0cm
area A = a $\times$ a = 6.0 $\times$ 6.0 = 36.0cm^2 from
equation 1
F = $\frac{\eta \times A \times x}{h}=\frac{4.4 \times 10^{10} \times 36 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.250 \times 10^{-3}}{6.01 \times 10^{-2}}$
F = 6.6 $\times$ 10^5N

questions.question: A piece of copper having a rectangular cross-section of 15.2 mm $ \times $ 19.1 mm is pulled in tension with 44,500 N force, producing only elastic deformation. Calculate the resulting strain. (Take Young's modulus of copper as 11 $ \times $10^10Pa)
questions.question_raw: A piece of copper having a rectangular cross-section of 15.2 mm {tex} \times {/tex} 19.1 mm is pulled in tension with 44,500 N force, producing only elastic deformation. Calculate the resulting strain. (Take Young's modulus of copper as 11 {tex} \times {/tex}1010Pa)
questions.explanation:
Given for copper cross-section = 15.2mm $\times$ 19.1mm
thus, cross-sectional area(A) = 15.2 $\times$ 19.1mm^2 = 2.9 $\times$ 10^−4m^2
restoring force tension(T) = 44500N
young modulus of copper (y) = 11 $\times$ 10^10pa
also $y=\frac{s t r e s s}{s t r a i n} $ $\Rightarrow$ strain = $\frac{s t r e s s}{y} $

questions.question: A steel cable with a radius of 1.5 cm supports a chairlift at a ski area. If the maximum stress is not to exceed 10^8 Nm^−2, what is the maximum load the cable can support?
questions.question_raw: A steel cable with a radius of 1.5 cm supports a chairlift at a ski area. If the maximum stress is not to exceed 108 Nm−2, what is the maximum load the cable can support?
questions.explanation:
Radius of the steel cable, r = 1.5 cm = 0.015 m
Maximum allowable stress = 10^8 Nm^-2
Maximum stress = $Maximum \ force \over Area \ of \ cross-section$
$\therefore$ Maximum force = Maximum stress $\times$ Area of cross-section
= 10^8 $\times $ $\pi$ (0.015)^2
= 7.065 $\times $ 10^4 N
Hence, the cable can support the maximum load of 7.07 $\times $ 10^4 N.

questions.question: A rigid bar of mass 15 kg is supported symmetrically by three wires each 2.0 m long. Those at each end are of copper and the middle one is of iron. Determine the ratios of their diameters if each is to have the same tension?
questions.question_raw: A rigid bar of mass 15 kg is supported symmetrically by three wires each 2.0 m long. Those at each end are of copper and the middle one is of iron. Determine the ratios of their diameters if each is to have the same tension?
questions.explanation:
Given condition is that tension in all three wires should be the same thus for the same tension, the extension $\Delta L$ in all three wires will be the same as the natural length of all three wires is the same i.e. 2.0 m, strain in all three wires will be equal. so young modulus of wires
$y=\frac{s t r e s s}{s t r a i n}=\frac{F \times L}{A \times \Delta L}=\frac{F \times L}{\pi r^{2} \times \Delta L} $ (D = 2r)
$y \propto \frac{1}{D^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{y_{\text {copper}}}{y_{\text {iron}}}=\frac{D^{2} \text { iron }}{D^{2} \text { copper }}$
$$$\frac{D_{\text {copper }}}{D_{\text {iron }}}=\sqrt{\frac{y_{\text {iron }}}{y_{\text {copper }}}}=\sqrt{\frac{19 \times 10^{10}}{12 \times 10^{10}}}$
$\frac{D_{\text {copper }}}{D_{\text {iron }}}$ = 1.25

questions.question: A 14.5 kg mass, fastened to the end of a steel wire of unstretched length 1.0 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with an angular velocity of 2 rev/s at the bottom of the circle. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.065 cm^2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of its path.
questions.question_raw: A 14.5 kg mass, fastened to the end of a steel wire of unstretched length 1.0 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with an angular velocity of 2 rev/s at the bottom of the circle. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.065 cm2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of its path.
questions.explanation:
given mass m = 14.5 kg
length of steel wire L = 1.0m
angular velocity $\omega$ = 2rev/s $\Rightarrow$ 2 $\times$ 2$\pi$rad/s = 4$\pi$rad/s cross sectional area of steel wire = 0.065cm^2 = 0.065 $\times$10^−4m^2
let us consider $\Delta$L be the elongation in steel wire when mass is at lowest point during its motion in vertical circle and T be the tension in wire apply in g_net force = centripetal force
T − mg = m$\omega$^2r
T = (14.5 $\times$ 9.8) + 14.5 $\times$ (4$\pi$)^2 $\times$ 1
T = 2429.5N now young modulus of steel wire y = 2.0 $\times$ 10^11pa
also $\mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{stress}}{\operatorname{strain}}=\frac{\mathrm{T} \times \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A} \times \Delta \mathrm{L}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{T} \times \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{y}}=\frac{2429.5 \times 1.0}{0.065 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11}}$
$\Delta$L = 1.9 $\times$ 10^−3m = 1.9mm

questions.question: Compute the bulk modulus of water from the following data: Initial volume = 100.0 litre, Pressure increase = 100.0 atm (1 atm = 1.013 $ \times $ 10^5 Pa), Final volume = 100.5 litre
questions.question_raw: Compute the bulk modulus of water from the following data: Initial volume = 100.0 litre, Pressure increase = 100.0 atm (1 atm = 1.013 {tex} \times {/tex} 105 Pa), Final volume = 100.5 litre
questions.explanation:
Bulk modulus is given by, B = $\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta {V\over V}}$
given $\Delta P$ = 100 atm = 100 $\times$ 1.013 $\times$ 10^5pa
V_INITIAL = 100.0 lit 
V_FINAL = 100.5 lit 
$\Delta$V = V_FINAL − V_INITIAL = 100.5 − 100.0 = 0.5
$B=\frac{100 \times 1.013 \times 10^{5}}{0.5 / 100.0}$
B = 2.026 $\times$ 10^9 pa

questions.question: What is the density of water at a depth where pressure is 80.0 atm, given that its density at the surface is 1.03 $ \times $ 10^3 kg m^−3?
questions.question_raw: What is the density of water at a depth where pressure is 80.0 atm, given that its density at the surface is 1.03 {tex} \times {/tex} 103 kg m−3?
questions.explanation:
Let the given depth be h.
Pressure at the given depth, p = 80.0 atm = 80 $\times$ 1.01 $\times$ 10^5 Pa
The density of water at the surface, ρ_1 = 1.03 $\times$ 10^3kg m^-3
Let ρ_2 be the density of water at the depth h.
Let V_1 be the volume of water of mass m at the surface.
Let V_2 be the volume of water of mass m at the depth h.
Let ΔV be the change in volume.
$\Delta V=V_1-V_2\;\;$ = $m\left[\frac1{\rho_1}-\frac1{\rho_2}\right]$
volumetric = strain = $\;\;\frac{\Delta V}{V_1}=m\left[\frac1{\rho_1}-\frac1{\rho_2}\right]\times\frac{\rho_1}m$
$ {\Delta V\over{ V_{1}}}={1-\frac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{2}} \quad }$$ \rightarrow$ (1) 
bulk modulus B = $\frac{\frac{P}{\Delta V}}{V_{1}} $
$\frac{\Delta V}{V_{1}}=\frac{P}{B}$ but compressibility 1 B of water is 45.8 $\times$ 10^-11pa^-1
△V/V_1=80×1.013×10^5×45.8×10^-11
 =3.71×10^-3 .....(ii)
For equations (i) and (ii), we get:
1−(ρ_1/ρ_2)=3.71×10^-3
ρ_2=1.03×10^3/[1−(3.71×10^-3)]
 =1.034×10^3kgm^-3
Therefore, the density of water at the given depth (h) is 1.034×10^3kgm^-3

questions.question: Compute the fractional change in volume of a glass slab, when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of 10 atm. The bulk modulus of glass 37 GPa?
questions.question_raw: Compute the fractional change in volume of a glass slab, when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of 10 atm. The bulk modulus of glass 37 GPa?
questions.explanation:
Bulk modulus is given by B = $\frac{\frac{P}{\Delta V}}{V}$
given B = 37 Gpa = 37 $\times$ 10^9pa
P = 10atm = 10 $\times$ 1.013 $\times$ 10^5atm
$\frac{\Delta V}{V}=\frac{P}{B}=\frac{10 \times 1.013 \times 10^{5}}{37 \times 10^{9}} $
$\frac{\Delta V}{V}=0.273 \times 10^{-4}$

questions.question: Determine the volume contraction of a solid copper cube, 10 cm on an edge, when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of 7.0 $ \times $ 10^6 Pa. The bulk modulus of copper 140 GPa?

Length of an edge of the solid copper cube, l = 10 cm = 0.1 m
Hydraulic pressure, p = 7.0 $\times$ 10^6 Pa
The bulk modulus of copper, B = 140 $\times$ 10^9 Pa
bulk modulus B = $\frac{P}{\frac{\Delta V}{V}}$

Bulk modulus B = $\Delta P \over {\Delta V\over V}$
given V = 1 lit
bulkmodulus B = 2.2Gpa = 2 .2 $\times$ 10^9pa

Flat faces at the narrow end of the anvil have a diameter of 0.50 mm, and the wide ends are subjected to a compression force of 50,000 N. What is the pressure at the tip of the anvil?
questions.explanation:
Given compression force F = 50000 N
Radius will be r = $\frac{diameter}{2}$ = $\frac{0.50}{2}$ = 0.25 mm = 0.25 $\times$ 10^-3 m
Area at which compression is applied A = $\pi$r^2 = 3.14 $\times$ (0.25 $\times$ 10^-3)^2 = 0.2 $\times$ 10^-6 m^2 
Pressure at the tip P = $\frac{Force}{area}$ = $\frac{50000}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}}$

questions.question: A rod of length 1.05 m having negligible mass is supported at its ends by two wires of steel (wire A) and aluminum (wire B) of equal lengths as shown in Figure. The cross-sectional areas of wires A and B are 1.0 ${\rm{m}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}$ and 2.0 mm^2, respectively. At what point along the rod should a mass m be suspended in order to produce equal stresses? Take Young's modulus of steel as 200 GPa, for aluminum 70 GPa.
questions.question_raw: A rod of length 1.05 m having negligible mass is supported at its ends by two wires of steel (wire A) and aluminum (wire B) of equal lengths as shown in Figure. The cross-sectional areas of wires A and B are 1.0 {tex}{\rm{m}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} and 2.0 mm2, respectively. At what point along the rod should a mass m be suspended in order to produce equal stresses? Take Young's modulus of steel as 200 GPa, for aluminum 70 GPa.
questions.explanation:
Let L be the length of each of the wires A and B. Also given cross-sectional area of wire A_steel = 1mm^2 cross sectional area of B_al = 2mm^2
young modulus for steel y_steel = 200 Gpa = 2 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2
young modulus of aluminium y_al = 70Gpa = 7 $\times$ 10^10 N/m^2
Let after placing the mass m weight on lower ends of wire be F_1 and F_2 then stress on wires A and B will be $\frac{F_1}{A_{steel}}$ and $\frac{F_2}{A_{al}}$
Now given condition is stress should be equal thus
$\frac{F_{1}}{A_{\text { steel }}}=\frac{F_{2}}{A_{a l}}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}=\frac{A_{s t e e l}}{A_{a l}} \rightarrow(1)$
if mass m is placed at a distance x and y from two wires then
F_1x = F_2y
$\frac{F_1}{F_2}$ = $\frac{y}{x}$ $\rightarrow$(2)
From equation 1 and 2
$\frac{y}{x}=\frac{A_{\text {steel}}}{A_{a l}} \Rightarrow x=\frac{A_{a l}}{A_{\text {steel}}} y $ $\rightarrow$ (3)
also given x + y = 1.05 (total length of rod)
y = 1.05 - x $\rightarrow$ (4)
thus from 3 and 4
$x=\frac{A_{a l}}{A_{s t e e l}}(1.05-x) $ $\Rightarrow x A_{s t e e l}=A_{a l} 1.05-A_{a l} x$
$x\left(A_{\text {steel}}+A_{a l}\right)=A_{a l} \times 1.05 $ $\Rightarrow x=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.05}{(2+1) \times 10^{-6}}$
x = 0.7 m 
Thus mass should be placed 0.7m from steel wire.

questions.question: A rod of length 1.05 m having negligible mass is supported at its ends by two wires of steel (wire A) and aluminum (wire B) of equal lengths as shown in Figure. The cross-sectional areas of wires A and B are 1.0 ${\rm{m}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}$ and 2.0 mm^2, respectively. At what point along the rod should a mass m be suspended in order to produce equal strains in both steel and aluminum wires. Take Young's modulus of steel as 200 GPa, for aluminum 70 GPa
questions.question_raw: A rod of length 1.05 m having negligible mass is supported at its ends by two wires of steel (wire A) and aluminum (wire B) of equal lengths as shown in Figure. The cross-sectional areas of wires A and B are 1.0 {tex}{\rm{m}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} and 2.0 mm2, respectively. At what point along the rod should a mass m be suspended in order to produce equal strains in both steel and aluminum wires. Take Young's modulus of steel as 200 GPa, for aluminum 70 GPa
questions.explanation:
Let L be the length of each wire. 
cross-sectional area of steel A_steel = 2mm^2
young modulus of steel y_steel = 200 Gpa= 2 $\times$ 10^11 pa
cross-sectional area of aluminum A_al = 1mm^2
young modulus of aluminium y_al = 70 Gpa =7 $\times$ 10^10 pa
Let after placing the mass m wight on lower ends of wire be F_1 and F_2 then stress on wires A and B will be $\frac{f_1}{A_{steel}}$ and $\frac{f_2}{A_{al}}$ 
Now given condition is strain should be equal thus
Young modulus y = $\frac{stress}{strain}$ $\Rightarrow$ strain = $\frac{stress}{y}$
Strain_st = $\frac{\operatorname{stress}_{s t}}{y_{steel}}=\frac{F_{1}}{A_{s t e e l} \times y_{s t e e l}}$ and strain_al = $\frac{stress_{al}}{y_{al}}$
for equal strains $\Rightarrow$ strain_st = strain_al
$\frac{F_{1}}{A_{s t e e l} \times y_{s t e e l}}=\frac{F_{2}}{A_{a l} \times y_{a l u m i n i u m}}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}=\frac{A_{\text {stel}} \times y_{\text { steel }}}{A_{\text {al}} \times y_{a l}} \rightarrow(1)$
if mass m is placed at a distance x and y from two wires then
F_1x = F_2y
$\frac{F_1}{F_2} $ = $\frac{y}{x}$ $\rightarrow$(2)
From equation 1 and 2
$\frac{y}{x}$ = $\frac{A_{\text {steel}} \times y_{\text {steel}}}{A_{\text {al}} \times y_{a l}}$ $\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{A_{a l} \times y_{a l}}{A_{s t e e l} \times y_{s t e e l}} y$ $\rightarrow$ (3)
also given x + y = 1.05 (total length of rod)
y = 1.05 - x $\Rightarrow$ (4)
thus from 3 and 4
x = $\frac{A_{a l} \times y_{a l}}{A_{s t e e l} \times y_{s t e e l}}(1.05-x)$ $\Rightarrow$ $x A_{s t e e l} \times y_{s t e e l}=A_{a l} y_{a l} 1.05-A_{a l} y_{a l} x$
$x\left(A_{s t e e l} y_{s t e el} +A_{a l} y_{a l}\right)=A_{a l} \times y_{a l} \times 1.05 \Rightarrow x=\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 7 \times 10^{10} \times 7 \times 10^{10} \times 1.05}{\left(2 \times 2 \times 10^{11}+1 \times 7 \times 10^{10}\right) \times 10^{-6}}$
x = 0.43 m
Thus mass should be placed 0.43 m from steel wire.

questions.question: A mild steel wire of length 1.0 m and cross-sectional area 0.50 $ \times $ 10^-2 cm^2 is stretched, well within its elastic limit, horizontally between two pillars. A mass of 100 g is suspended from the mid-point of the wire. Calculate the depression at the midpoint.
questions.question_raw: A mild steel wire of length 1.0 m and cross-sectional area 0.50 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-2 cm2 is stretched, well within its elastic limit, horizontally between two pillars. A mass of 100 g is suspended from the mid-point of the wire. Calculate the depression at the midpoint.
questions.explanation:
given length of wire L = 1.0 m
area A = 0.50 $\times$ 10^-2cm^2 = 0.5 $\times$ 10^−6m^2
mass suspended = 100g = 0.1 kg 
using standard value for young modulus of steely = 2.0 $\times$ 10^11N/m^2
depression at midpoint $x ={ l ({Mg\over {YA}})}^{1/3}$
$x = 0.5 ({0.1 \times 10 \over (2 \times 10 ^11)(0.50 \times 10 ^{-6})})^{1/3}$
x = 1.074 $\times$ 10^-2 m

questions.question: Two strips of metal are riveted together at their ends by four rivets, each of diameter 6.0 mm. What is the maximum tension that can be exerted by the riveted strip if the shearing stress on the rivet is not to exceed 6.9 $ \times $ 10^7 Pa? Assume that each rivet is to carry one-quarter of the load:
questions.question_raw: Two strips of metal are riveted together at their ends by four rivets, each of diameter 6.0 mm. What is the maximum tension that can be exerted by the riveted strip if the shearing stress on the rivet is not to exceed 6.9 {tex} \times {/tex} 107 Pa? Assume that each rivet is to carry one-quarter of the load:
questions.explanation:
if the riveted strip is subjected to a stretching load W, the tensile force i.e. tension in each strip(equal to W)
let A be the area of each rivet on which the shearing force acts.
shearing stress on each rivet = $\frac{\text {shearing force}}{\text {area}}$= $\frac{W}{4 A}$
let W_max be the maximum permissible tension exerted by the riveted strip then $\frac{W_{\max }}{4 A}$ = $6.9 \times 10^{7} \Rightarrow W_{\max }$ = 4 A $\times$ 6.9 $\times$ 10^7 $\Rightarrow$ (1)
Given that diameter of each rivet d = 6.0 mm
area A = $\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}=\frac{\pi \mathrm{d}^{2}}{4}=\frac{3.14 \times\left(6 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{4}$
A = 28.26 $\times$ 10^−6m^2
substitute in equation 1
W_max = 4 $\times$ 28.26 $\times$ 10^−6 $\times$ 6.9 $\times$ 10^7
W_max = 7.8 $\times$ 10^3 N

questions.question: The Marina trench is located in the Pacific Ocean, and in one place it is nearly eleven km beneath the surface of the water. The water pressure at the bottom of the trench is about 1.1 $ \times $ 10^8 Pa. A steel ball of initial volume 0.32 m^3 is dropped into the ocean and falls to the bottom of the trench. What is the change in the volume of the ball when it reaches the bottom? The bulk modulus of steel = 160 GPa.
questions.question_raw: The Marina trench is located in the Pacific Ocean, and in one place it is nearly eleven km beneath the surface of the water. The water pressure at the bottom of the trench is about 1.1 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 Pa. A steel ball of initial volume 0.32 m3 is dropped into the ocean and falls to the bottom of the trench. What is the change in the volume of the ball when it reaches the bottom? The bulk modulus of steel = 160 GPa.
questions.explanation:
bulk modulus is given by $B=\frac{\Delta P}{\frac{\Delta V}{V}} \Rightarrow \Delta V=\frac{\Delta P V}{B}$ $\Rightarrow$ (1)
volume of ball = 0.32 m^3
given water pressure at bottom = 1.1 $\times $ 10^8 pa
depth of marina trench = 11000 km bulk modulus of ball B = 160 Gpa = 160 $\times$ 10^9 pa
pressure at top of surface will be atmospheic pressure p_0 = P_atm
pressure at bottom will be p_2 = P_atm + P_water = P_atm + (1.1 $\times$ 10^8)
$\Delta P=p_{2}-p_{1}$ = 1.1 $\times$ 10^8pa
substitute values in equation 1
$\Delta V=\frac{1.1 \times 10^{8} \times 0.32}{160 \times 10^{9}}$
$\Delta V=2.2 \times 10^{-4} $m^3

questions.question: What diameter should a 10-m-long steel wire have if we do not want it to stretch more than 0.5 cm under a tension of 940 N? Take Young's modulus of steel as 20 $ \times $ 10^10 Pa.
questions.question_raw: What diameter should a 10-m-long steel wire have if we do not want it to stretch more than 0.5 cm under a tension of 940 N? Take Young's modulus of steel as 20 {tex} \times {/tex} 1010 Pa.
questions.explanation:
Given length of wire L = 10 m
elongation in wire $\Delta L$ = 0.5 cm = 0.5 $\times$ 10^-2 m
restoring force in wire i.e. tension T = 940 N
young modulus of wire Y = 20 $\times$ 10^10N/m^2
using relation for young modulus $Y = \frac{s t r e s s}{s t r a i n} = \frac{T \times L}{A \times \Delta L}$
$A = \frac{T \times L}{Y \times \Delta L} = \frac{940 \times 10}{20 \times 10^{10} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-2}}$
$\pi \mathrm{R}^{2} = 940 \times 10^{-8}$
R = 17.30 $\times$ 10^-4
Diameter d = 2 R $\Rightarrow$ 2 $\times$ 17.30 $\times$ 10^-4
d = 34.60 $\times$ 10^-4 m $\approx$ 3.4 mm

questions.question: A solid brass sphere is initially surrounded by air, and the air pressure exerted on it is 1.0 $ \times $ 10^5N/m^2 (normal atmospheric pressure). The sphere is lowered into the ocean to a depth at which the pressure is 2.0 $ \times $ 10^7 N/m^2. The volume of the sphere in the air is 0.50 m^3. By how much does this volume change once the sphere is submerged? ( take the modulus of brass as 61 GPa.)
questions.question_raw: A solid brass sphere is initially surrounded by air, and the air pressure exerted on it is 1.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 105N/m2 (normal atmospheric pressure). The sphere is lowered into the ocean to a depth at which the pressure is 2.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 107 N/m2. The volume of the sphere in the air is 0.50 m3. By how much does this volume change once the sphere is submerged? ( take the modulus of brass as 61 GPa.)
questions.explanation:
Given initial pressure p_1 = 1.0 $\times$ 10^5 N/m^2
final pressure p_2 = 2.0 $\times$ 10^7 N/m^2
volume of sphere V = 0.50 m^3
bulk modulus B of brass = 61 Gpa = $61 \times 10^{9}$ pa
bulk modulus is given by $B=-\frac{\Delta p}{\frac{\Delta V}{V}}$
$\Delta V=-\frac{\Delta p \times V}{B}=-\frac{\left(p_{2}-p_{1}\right) \times V}{B}$ = $-\frac{\left(2.0 \times 10^{7}-1.0 \times 10^{5}\right) \times 0.50}{61 \times 10^{9}} $
$\Delta V=-1.6 \times 10^{-4} m^3$

questions.question: A 200-kg load is hung on a wire with a length of 4.00 m, a cross-sectional area of 0.200 $ \times $ 10^−4 m^2, and Young’s modulus of 8.00 $ \times $ 10^10 N/m^2. What is its increase in length?
questions.question_raw: A 200-kg load is hung on a wire with a length of 4.00 m, a cross-sectional area of 0.200 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−4 m2, and Young’s modulus of 8.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 1010 N/m2. What is its increase in length?
questions.explanation:
Given load hung to a wire = 200 kg thus force applied to wire = weight W = 200 $\times$ g
W = 200 $\times$ 9.8 = 1960 N
at equilibrium restoring force = applied force
thus, tension T = 1960 N
length of wire L = 4.0 m
cross sectional area of wire = 0.200 $\times$ 10^-4 m^2
young modulus of wire Y = 8.00 $\times$ 10^10 N/m^2
also $Y = \frac{stress}{strain} = \frac{T \times L}{A \times \Delta L} $
$\Delta L$ = $\frac{T \times L}{A \times Y} = \frac{1960 \times 4.0}{0.200 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.00 \times 10^{10}} $
$\Delta L$ = 4.90 mm

questions.question: A child slides across a floor in a pair of rubber-soled shoes. The frictional force acting on each foot is 20.0 N. The footprint area of each shoe’s sole is 14.0 cm^2, and the thickness of each sole is 5.00 mm. Find the horizontal distance by which the upper and lower surfaces of each sole are offset. The shear modulus of the rubber is 3.00 $ \times $ 10^6 N/m^2.
questions.question_raw: A child slides across a floor in a pair of rubber-soled shoes. The frictional force acting on each foot is 20.0 N. The footprint area of each shoe’s sole is 14.0 cm2, and the thickness of each sole is 5.00 mm. Find the horizontal distance by which the upper and lower surfaces of each sole are offset. The shear modulus of the rubber is 3.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 N/m2.
questions.explanation:
let restoring force be T
at equilibrium frictional force = restoring force
thus T = 20 N
area A of each sole = 14.0 cm^2 = 14.0 $\times$ 10^-4 m^2
thickness of each sole H = 5.00 mm = 5.00 $\times$ 10^-3 m
shear modulus of rubber $\eta$ = 3.00 $\times$ 10^6 N/m^2
let distance by which upper and lower surface are offset is x
shear modulus $\eta$ = $\frac{\text {shear stress}}{\text {shear strain}} $ = $ \frac{T \times H}{A \times x}$
x = $\frac{T \times H}{A \times \eta}$ = $\frac{20 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{14 \times 10^{-4} \times 3 \times 10^{6}}$= 2.38 mm

questions.question: If the elastic limit of copper is 1.50 $ \times $ 10^8 N/m^2, determine the minimum diameter a copper wire can have under a load of 10.0 kg if its elastic limit is not to be exceeded?
questions.question_raw: If the elastic limit of copper is 1.50 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 N/m2, determine the minimum diameter a copper wire can have under a load of 10.0 kg if its elastic limit is not to be exceeded?
questions.explanation:
Given mass to be hung m = 10.0 kg
elastic limit = 1.50 $\times$ 10^8 N/m^2
elastic limit i.e. pessure P = $\frac{\text {force}}{\text {area}} $ $\rightarrow$ (1)
force F = m g and area = $\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$ (r $\rightarrow$ radius of wire)
using 1
1.50 $\times$ 10^8= $\frac{10.0 \times 9.8}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}$
r^2 = 20.80 $\times$ 10^-8
r = 4.56 $\times$ 10^-4 m
d = 2r
d = 0.912 mm

questions.question: If the shear stress exceeds about 4.00 $ \times $ 10^8 N/m^2, steel ruptures. Determine the shearing force necessary to shear a steel bolt 1.00 cm in diameter?
questions.question_raw: If the shear stress exceeds about 4.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 N/m2, steel ruptures. Determine the shearing force necessary to shear a steel bolt 1.00 cm in diameter?
questions.explanation:
Given shear stress S = 4.00 $\times$ 10^8 N/m^2
diameter d = 1.00 cm
radius r = $d\over 2 $= 0.5cm
area (A) = $\pi$r^2 = 3.14 $\times$ (0.5 $\times$ 10^−2)^2 = 0.785 $\times$ 10^−4m^2
stress S = $force \over area$ $\Rightarrow$ force = S $\times$ area
force F = 4.00 $\times$ 10^8 $\times$ 0.785 $\times$ 10^−4 F = 3.14 $\times$ 10^4N

questions.question: If the shear stress exceeds about 4.00 $ \times $ 10^8 N/m^2, steel ruptures. Determine the shearing force necessary to punch a 1.00-cm-diameter hole in a steel plate 0.500 cm thick?
questions.question_raw: If the shear stress exceeds about 4.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 N/m2, steel ruptures. Determine the shearing force necessary to punch a 1.00-cm-diameter hole in a steel plate 0.500 cm thick?
questions.explanation:
Given shear stress S = 4.00 $\times$ 10^8 N/m^2
diameter d = 1.00 cm
radius r = $d\over 2$ = 0.5cm
height h = 0.500 cm
force will be applied on area A = 2 $\pi$rh = 2 $\times $ 3.14 $\times $ (0.5 $\times$ 10^-2) $\times$ 0.5 $\times$ 10^-2 = 1.57 $\times$ 10^-4 m
area A = $\pi$r^2 = 3.14 $\times$ (0.5 $\times$ 10^−2)^2 = 0.785 $\times$ 10^−4m^2
stress S = $force \over area$ $\Rightarrow$ force = S $\times$ area
force F = 4.00 $\times$ 10^8 $\times$ 1.57$\times$ 10^−4 
F = 6.28$\times$ 10^4N

questions.question: When water freezes, it expands by about 9.00%. What would be the pressure increase inside your automobile’s engine block if the water in it froze? (The bulk modulus of ice is 2.00 $ \times $ 10^9 N/m^2.)
questions.question_raw: When water freezes, it expands by about 9.00%. What would be the pressure increase inside your automobile’s engine block if the water in it froze? (The bulk modulus of ice is 2.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 109 N/m2.)
questions.explanation:
Given $\frac{\Delta V}{V}$ = 9% = $\frac{9}{100}$
the bulk modulus of water = 2.00 $\times$ 10 ^9 N/m^2
$B=\frac{\Delta P}{\frac{\Delta V}{V}}$ $\Rightarrow $ $\Delta P=B \times \frac{\Delta V}{V}$
$ΔP$ = 2.00 $\times$ 10^9 $\times$ $9\over 100$

questions.question: In a materials testing laboratory, a metal wire made from a new alloy is found to break when a tensile force of 90.8 N is applied perpendicular to each end. If the diameter of the wire is 1.84 mm, what is the breaking stress of the alloy?
questions.question_raw: In a materials testing laboratory, a metal wire made from a new alloy is found to break when a tensile force of 90.8 N is applied perpendicular to each end. If the diameter of the wire is 1.84 mm, what is the breaking stress of the alloy?
questions.explanation:
given tensile force F = 90.8 N
diameter d = 1.84 mm
radius r = 1.84/2 = 0.92 mm = 0.92 $\times$10^−3m
stress = $\frac{\text {tensile force}}{\text {area}}=\frac{90.8}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}=\frac{90.8}{3.14 \times\left(0.92 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}$
stress = 3.41 $\times$ 10^7 N/m^2

questions.question: A steel cable with cross-sectional area 3.00 cm^2 has an elastic limit of 2.40 $ \times $ 10^8 Pa. Find the maximum upward acceleration that can be given a 1200-kg elevator supported by the cable if the stress is not to exceed one-third of the elastic limit?
questions.question_raw: A steel cable with cross-sectional area 3.00 cm2 has an elastic limit of 2.40 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 Pa. Find the maximum upward acceleration that can be given a 1200-kg elevator supported by the cable if the stress is not to exceed one-third of the elastic limit?
questions.explanation:
Given area of cable A = 3cm^2
elastic limit = breaking stress = 2.40 $\times$ 10^8pa mass of elevator m = 1200kg
given condition stress should not exceed $\frac13$ of elastic limit i.e.
$\text {stress}<\frac{\text {elastic limit}}{3}$
$\frac{\text {force}}{\text {area}}<\frac{2.4 \times 10^{8}}{3}$
force < $\frac{2.4 \times 10^{8} \times 3 \times 10^{-4}}{3}$
force < 2.4 $\times$ 10^4
but force = mass $\times$ acceleration 
net acceleration a_net^ = $\frac{2.4 \times 10^{4}}{1200}$
a_net = 20m/s^2 
when elevator moves upwards net acceleration = a + g
here 'a' is acceleration given to elevator and'g' is acceleration due to gravity
a + g = 20
a = 20 - 9.8
a = 10.2 m/s^2

questions.question: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched if the wire were twice as long?
questions.question_raw: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched if the wire were twice as long?
questions.explanation:
Let length in the first case is L
extension $\Delta L$ = 0.18 mm
length in 2 nd case will be 2L
given that stress $\alpha$ strain $\Rightarrow $ stress = k $\times$ strain (k is proportionality constant)
as stress in both cases is same because same flood lamp is hung and
the diameter of wire remains same on increasing length to 2L thus force per unit area
i.e. stress will be the same. so
$\frac{\text {stress}_{1}}{\text {stress}_{2}}=\frac{k \times \text {strain}_{1}}{k \times \text {strain}_{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\operatorname{strain}_{1}}{\operatorname{strain}_{2}}$ = 1 (stress_1 = stress_2)
$\frac{\frac{\Delta L_{1}}{L_{1}}}{\frac{\Delta L_{2}}{L_{2}}}=1 \Rightarrow \Delta L_{2}=\frac{\Delta L_{1} \times L_{2}}{L_{1}}=\frac{0.18 \times 2 L}{L}$
$\Delta L_2$ = 0.36 mm

questions.question: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched if the wire had the same length but twice the diameter?
questions.question_raw: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched if the wire had the same length but twice the diameter?
questions.explanation:
let length in the first case is L
extension $\Delta L$ = 0.18 mm
length in 2nd case will be 2L
given that stress $\alpha$ strain ...(1)
as stress in both cases is same because the diameter of wire is changed so the area of wire will change thus force per unit area
i.e. stress will be the same. so
stress = force/area
from (1)st equation :-
$$${F\over A} \propto {\Delta{L\over l}}$
${1\over A}\propto \Delta l$ ( because l is same )
${1\over r^2} \propto \Delta l$
$({r_2\over r_1})^2 = {\Delta l_1\over \Delta l_2}$
thus, $({2r\over r})^2 = {0.18\over \Delta l_2}$
$\Delta L_2$ = 0.045 mm

questions.question: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched for a copper wire of the original length and diameter? (young modulus of steel=2.0 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2 and young modulus of copper=1.1 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2)
questions.question_raw: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched for a copper wire of the original length and diameter? (young modulus of steel=2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 N/m2  and young modulus of copper=1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 N/m2)
questions.explanation:
Let the length of wire in both cases be L
extension in case of steel $\Delta L_{\text {steel}}$ = 0.18 mm
young modulus y = $\frac{\text {stress}}{\text {strain}}$
as stress in both case is same
$y_{\text {steel}}=\frac{\text {stress}}{\text {strain}_{\text {steel}}} \Rightarrow$ (1) 
$y_{\text {copper}}=\frac{\text {stress}}{\text {strain}_{\text {copper}}} \Rightarrow$ (2)
equation 1 divided by equation 
$$$\frac{y_{\text {steel }}}{y_{\text {copper }}}=\frac{\text { strain }_{\text {copper }}}{\text { strain }_{\text {steel }}}$
$\frac{2.0 \times 10^{11}}{1.1 \times 10^{11}}=\frac{\frac{\Delta L_{\text {copper}}}{L}}{\frac{\Delta L_{\text {steel}}}{L}}$
$\Delta L_{\text {copper}}=\frac{2.0 \times 0.18}{1.1}$
$\Delta L_{\text {copper}}$ = 0.327 mm $\approx$ 0.33 mm

questions.question: A 12.0-kg mass, fastened to the end of aluminum wire with an unstretched length of 0.50 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with a constant angular speed of 120 rev/min. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.014 cm^2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of the path (young modulus of steel = 0.70 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2)?
questions.question_raw: A 12.0-kg mass, fastened to the end of aluminum wire with an unstretched length of 0.50 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with a constant angular speed of 120 rev/min. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.014 cm2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of the path (young modulus of steel = 0.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 N/m2)?
questions.explanation:
Given mass m = 12.0 kg
length of aluminium wire L = 0.50 m
angular speed $\omega$ = 120 rev/min = 120 $\times$ $\frac{2 \pi}{60} = 4 \pi$ rad/s
area of wire A = 0.014 cm^2 = 0.014 $\times$ 10^-4m^2
when whirled in vertical circle at lowest point let tension in wire be T
applying net force = centripetal force
T - mg = $\mathrm{m} \omega^{2} \mathrm{r}$
T= (12 $\times$ (4$\pi$)^2 $\times$0.50) + 12 $\times$ 9.8
T = 1064.12N
young modulus y = $\frac{\mathrm{stress}}{\mathrm{strain}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{A}}}{\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{T} \times \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{y}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{1064.12 \times 0.50}{0.014 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.70 \times 10^{11}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}$ = 54291.91 $\times$ 10^-7 m = 0.54 cm

The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.014 cm2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the highest point of its path. questions.explanation:
given mass m = 12.0 kg

area of wire A = 0.014 cm^2 = 0.014 $\times$ 10^-4m^2
when whirled in vertical circle at lowest point let tension in wire be T
applying net force = centripetal force
T - mg = $\mathrm{m} \omega^{2} \mathrm{r}$
T=(12 $\times$ (4$\pi$)^2 $\times$0.50) + 12 $\times$ 9.8
